docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Steel Crown Historical Fencing

Historical European Martial Arts · Charlotte, NC

About the Club

Steel Crown Historical Fencing is a HEMA organization in Charlotte, North Carolina dedicated to the serious study and practice of historical fencing. We train longsword and other weapons drawn from primary historical sources, with an emphasis on disciplined technique, honest instruction, and a culture where every member is both a student and a contributor.

Whether you are new to HEMA or an experienced practitioner, you will find a training environment built on mutual respect, open knowledge, and long-term development.

docs.title: What We Do
docs.text:
• Longsword

    Primary weapon and focus of all training, from foundational technique to competitive application.

  • Competition

    We train to compete. Sparring culture and curriculum are built around tournament preparation.

  • Training Culture

    Honest feedback, shared knowledge, mutual growth. No gatekeeping, no ego.

  • Study Materials

    Full curriculum documentation for Fiore dei Liberi and Godinho Montante, available to all members.

docs.title: Materials
docs.text:
Our study materials cover the historical sources we train from, with full documentation of guards, strikes, and tactical concepts.

Fiore for HEMA Fencers Godinho Montante

docs.title: Liability Waiver
docs.text:
All participants must complete a liability waiver before training with Steel Crown Historical Fencing.

Complete the Waiver

docs.title: Mission Statement
docs.text: Steel Crown Historical Fencing is a Historical European Martial Arts (HEMA) organization dedicated to the study, practice, and advancement of historical fencing through disciplined training, integrity, and shared growth. We foster a culture where every member, regardless of experience, is both a student and a contributor, united by sportsmanship, mutual respect, and the pursuit of continual improvement. Through instruction, competition, historical study, and community engagement, we aim to develop skilled fencers, responsible training partners, and strong communities built on trust and accountability.

docs.title: Vision Statement
docs.text: We envision a club where excellence and community reinforce one another, and where rigorous training, historical study, and genuine camaraderie exist side by side. Steel Crown Historical Fencing strives to build an environment in which every student is challenged, supported, and valued, and where knowledge is shared openly across all levels of experience. We measure success not only through individual achievement, but through the strength, character, and development of the club as a whole. Our goal is to build a lasting institution that develops exceptional fencers, respectful competitors, dedicated contributors, and people of character.

docs.title: Core Organizational Principles
docs.text:
• Student-Centered Growth

    We believe every member is responsible for learning and contributing, and that growth is a shared responsibility.

  • Integrity in Instruction

    We value honesty, integrity, and transparency in how we teach, train, and represent the art.

  • Excellence Through Discipline

    We believe that skill is built through consistency, accountability, and purposeful training.

  • Safety and Responsibility

    We are responsible for creating and maintaining a safe and inclusive training environment that allows for integrity, trust, and long-term development.

  • Respect

    We treat everyone, including ourselves, with kindness and respect.

  • Sportsmanship

    We claim victory with humility and accept defeat with dignity.

  • Community Over Ego

    We believe having a strong sense of community is more important than any individual status, recognition, or organizational politics.

  • Open Knowledge

    We believe that knowledge should be shared freely and carried forward for the benefit of the entire club.

  • Long-Term Development

    We aim to build not only skilled competitors, but capable leaders and ambassadors for HEMA.

docs.title: Fencing Concepts
docs.text:
← Back to Materials

Techniques are specific. Principles are general.

This section covers foundational concepts that apply across weapon systems and historical traditions. Understanding these principles gives you a framework for reading exchanges, diagnosing why something failed, and making better decisions under pressure, regardless of what you are holding.

The concepts here are not alternatives to the weapon-specific curricula. They are the underlying logic that makes those curricula coherent. A student who understands lines, openings, opposition, and structure will learn specific techniques faster and retain them better.

docs.title: What's Inside
docs.text:
The Four Openings — How the body is divided into targets, and why that vocabulary matters for every exchange.

Inside and Outside Line — The most practically useful spatial concept in fencing. Where your weapon is relative to your opponent's determines what options are available to both of you.

Opposition and Cover — The difference between deflecting an attack and controlling the line. Why some defenses hold and others don't.

Structure — What makes a position strong. The difference between a blade that looks threatening and one that actually is.

docs.title: Inside and Outside Line
docs.text:
Where your weapon is relative to your opponent's weapon determines what both of you can do.

This is not a subtle point. It is probably the single most practically useful spatial concept in fencing. Students who understand it make better decisions in sparring immediately. Students who do not understand it repeat the same errors indefinitely without knowing why.

Your position relative to your opponent's weapon is the first thing to read in any exchange.

docs.title: The Definition
docs.text:
The inside line is the space between your opponent's weapon and their centerline. If your blade is on the inside line, it is positioned between their guard and the center of their body.

The outside line is the space on the far side of their weapon arm. If your blade is on the outside line, it is positioned away from their centerline, on the side of their weapon hand or beyond it.

A simpler way to feel the distinction: on the inside line, your blade and their blade are in the same general space. On the outside line, your blade has cleared their weapon entirely and is approaching from a direction they cannot easily address with it.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
From the inside line, both weapons are competing for the same space. Your attack and their parry are likely to meet. Your thrust goes toward the center of their body, but their blade can reach it. You can threaten directly, but so can they.

From the outside line, their weapon cannot easily reach your blade without a significant change of position. You are attacking from an angle their guard does not naturally cover. This is why gaining the outside line is a central goal in most fencing systems — not because it is safe, but because it limits what the opponent can do in response.

The Exchange of Thrusts works precisely because the offline step moves your body to the outside line while your blade returns on the inside. Your target is exposed. Theirs is not.

docs.title: How Lines Change
docs.text:
Lines are not fixed. They shift constantly as the exchange develops.

A step offline can move you from the inside to the outside line in a single motion. A crossing of the blades can reverse which fencer holds which line. A beat or deflection that moves the opponent's blade past center puts them on the outside line.

The goal is not to be on the outside line once. It is to understand which line you currently hold and make decisions accordingly.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Attacking from the inside line without controlling the opponent's weapon. This is the most common beginner error. The attack is direct and the target is real, but the opponent's weapon can reach you just as directly. Both fencers hit each other. The solution is either to move to the outside line first, or to control the opponent's weapon before committing.

Gaining the outside line and then not committing. A student steps offline, has a clear outside line, and then hesitates or reverts to a technique that works from the inside. The advantage evaporates because it was not used.

Confusing outside line with wide measure. The outside line is about the angle of your weapon relative to theirs, not about how far away you are. You can be on the outside line at stretto distance. A disarm from Fiore's stretto section often requires being on the outside line of the weapon arm at extremely close range.

docs.title: Connection to the Fiore Curriculum
docs.text:
Every largo play in the Fiore curriculum involves a line decision.

Breaking the Thrust gains the outside line by stepping left while beating the blade to that side. The Peasant's Blow exits the inside line when the opponent's strike carries past center, leaving the Scholar on the effective outside of a now-committed attack.

In stretto, the joint locks require an outside position on the weapon arm specifically. The Lower Bind, Middle Bind, and Upper Bind all fail if applied from the inside. The outside position is not a preference, it is a mechanical requirement.

Reading the plays with this concept in mind makes the spatial logic of each technique clear.

docs.title: Opposition and Cover
docs.text:
Not all defenses are equal.

A parry that deflects an attack has stopped one threat. A parry that controls the line has stopped all threats from that direction while creating an immediate counter-opportunity. These are different actions with different outcomes, and the distinction between them matters every time blades meet.

Opposition closes a line with your blade while your point threatens. Cover only closes the line.

docs.title: The Distinction
docs.text:
Cover is a defensive action that moves an incoming weapon off its target. The attack is deflected. The line is cleared for that moment. But nothing else is established. The opponent can recover their blade, change lines, and attack again. Cover buys time.

Opposition is a defensive action that controls the incoming weapon by maintaining contact and pressure while your own point angles toward the opponent's target. The attack is not just deflected — it is held. The opponent cannot recover their blade freely because yours is against it and your weight is behind it. Opposition closes one threat and creates another simultaneously.

The Exchange of Thrusts is the clearest example in the curriculum. The offline step takes your body away from the incoming thrust. The crossing of the blades is not a deflection — your arms extend low and outward to collect the incoming blade while your point rises toward the opponent's face. Both happen in one motion. The attack is opposed, not just parried. You are threatening before their attack has finished.

docs.title: What Makes Opposition Work
docs.text:
Opposition requires three things to be present at the same time:

Contact with the incoming weapon. Your blade must be against theirs with enough pressure to control its path. Light contact deflects. Maintained contact controls.

Weight behind the blade. Opposition is not an arm movement. The force that holds the opponent's blade in check comes from body alignment and weight transfer, not from grip strength. A fencer who opposes with the arms alone will be pushed off by a stronger opponent. A fencer who opposes with their structure behind the blade will hold the line regardless of size difference.

Point threatening a target. If your blade controls theirs but your point is aimed at the ceiling, you have cover, not opposition. The point must be on a line that forces the opponent to respond to it. This is what makes opposition a simultaneous action — defense and offense exist at the same moment.

docs.title: Cover Has Its Place
docs.text:
Cover is not wrong. There are situations where deflection is the correct choice.

Breaking the Thrust uses a beat, which is closer to a forceful cover than a true opposition. The blade is knocked aside and the body moves to exploit the moment before it recovers. This is two-time: cover, then counter. It works because the body movement is fast enough that the gap between the two actions is negligible.

The distinction is that opposition collapses the two actions into one. When one-time action is available, it is preferable. When the geometry or timing makes it unavailable, two-time cover and counter is the correct response.

Training to prefer opposition when possible builds the habit of simultaneous action. Training to accept cover when necessary builds tactical flexibility.

docs.title: Opposition in Stretto
docs.text:
Opposition continues to matter at close range, though it changes form.

In stretto, the weapons are no longer crossing near the tips. The contact is mid-blade or at the hilt. The mechanical principle is the same: pressure against the opponent's weapon arm or blade while your own action continues toward its target.

The pommel strike is an opposition in this sense. The bind is the contact. The roll under the bind and the strike of the pommel happen together. The blade controls the line while the pommel acts.

docs.title: In Practice
docs.text:
When reviewing a defensive action that failed, ask whether it was cover or opposition.

If the opponent hit you after your parry, the parry may have been cover that deflected without controlling. Their blade recovered before yours returned to threaten. The fix is not a harder parry. It is an earlier point — threatening before they can recover.

If your opposition failed, the cause is almost always weight. The arms held the line but the body was not behind them. The fix is to feel where your mass is when you make contact with the blade, and make sure it is moving toward the opponent, not away.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
What Makes a Position Strong

A blade can be pointed at the opponent and still be no threat.

A guard can look correct and still fold under the first pressure. A thrust can reach its target and still be pushed aside. The difference in each case is structure: whether the body position behind the technique supports it or undermines it.

A line without structure is a suggestion. A line with structure is a fact.

docs.title: What Structure Is
docs.text:
Structure is the alignment of your body behind your weapon.

When your blade is extended and your shoulder, hip, and foot are aligned to support it, force applied against your blade travels into your skeleton rather than collapsing your arm. Your position is load-bearing. An opponent who pushes against it is pushing against your weight.

When your blade is extended but your elbow is bent outward, your shoulder is dropped, or your weight is on the wrong foot, force applied against your blade bypasses your skeleton and goes directly into your muscles. Muscles fatigue and fail. Structure holds.

This is why Fiore's guards look the way they do. They are not aesthetic choices. Each guard positions the body so that the weapon is backed by weight and alignment. The guard is strong not because the fencer grips harder, but because the geometry makes it load-bearing.

docs.title: The Relationship Between Line and Structure
docs.text:
Line describes where your weapon is pointed. Structure describes whether your body supports it.

You can have line without structure: blade aimed at the opponent, weight on the back foot, shoulder floating. The line exists but collapses on contact.

You can have structure without a threatening line: weight forward, body aligned, blade pointed at the ground. The position is stable but not dangerous.

What you want is both: a blade on a threatening line with your weight and alignment behind it. This is what Fiore's master figures demonstrate. The guard is positioned so that extending from it naturally produces both.

docs.title: How Structure Fails
docs.text:
Weight on the wrong foot. A thrust with your weight on the rear foot has no mass behind it. The blade reaches but the body does not follow. Against any resistance, the thrust deflects. The fix is to feel whether your weight is moving toward your target as the blade extends.

Collapsed shoulder. When the sword shoulder drops or rotates inward, the alignment from hand to shoulder to torso breaks. Force against the blade now levers against a bent joint rather than traveling into the skeleton. Shoulder alignment is one of the most common structural errors in both guards and extended positions.

Elbow lifted or floating. In a guard or extended position, an elbow that is not supported by the structure underneath it creates a weak point. The blade may be on the correct line, but pressure against it bends the arm at the elbow rather than transmitting into the body.

Over-reaching. Extending further than your structure can support. The blade reaches the target but the body is stretched beyond stability. The position looks like it has line but has sacrificed the structural support that makes the line meaningful.

docs.title: Structure Under Pressure
docs.text:
The clearest test of structure is contact.

In a static drill, almost any position looks reasonable. Under pressure — a partner pushing against your blade, applying a bind, or trying to displace your guard — structural weaknesses become immediately apparent. A guard that folds when pushed was never a guard; it was a shape.

This is why pressure testing is part of learning any technique. The drill teaches the position. The pressure reveals whether the structure is present. If the position collapses, the feedback is not that the technique is wrong. It is that the structure is missing.

docs.title: Connection to the Fiore Curriculum
docs.text:
The three joint locks demonstrate structure most clearly from the receiving end.

When the ligadura mezana is applied correctly, the structural failure is not in the arm being grabbed. It is in the elbow joint reaching its mechanical limit. No amount of grip strength or muscle tension overcomes a joint at its limit. The lock exploits a structural vulnerability.

Conversely, the scholar who applies the lock must have structure behind the pressing motion. The lock fails when the scholar's own alignment breaks: arm without body weight, weight on the back foot, shoulder not supporting the press.

The same principle applies in every largo exchange. A guard that holds when the opponent's blade makes contact is structurally sound. One that deflects or collapses is not, regardless of how correct it looked before contact was made.

docs.title: In Practice
docs.text:
When a technique fails under pressure, structure is usually the first place to look.

Ask: was my weight moving toward the target, or away from it? Was my shoulder aligned, or floating? Was the contact point of the technique load-bearing, or was I relying on muscle tension to hold the position?

Structure is built slowly through deliberate practice with feedback. Drilling in front of a mirror, working with a partner who provides controlled resistance, and reviewing video of sparring are all useful. The goal is to build positions that hold under contact automatically, without conscious attention to alignment, so that attention is free for reading the exchange.

docs.title: The Four Openings
docs.text:
Quattro Partimenti

Every attack lands somewhere. Every defense covers something.

Fencing vocabulary without a system for describing the body quickly becomes imprecise. "He hit me" tells you nothing useful. "He took the upper left opening after I crossed wide" tells you exactly what happened and why.

The four openings give you that system.

The body has four targets. Every technique either threatens one or closes one.

docs.title: The Division
docs.text:
The body is divided into four quadrants by two lines: one horizontal at the waist, one vertical through the centerline.

Upper right — Above the waist, on your opponent's right side (your left as you face them). Includes the right shoulder, neck, and head.

Upper left — Above the waist, on your opponent's left side. Includes the left shoulder and the same targets from the other angle.

Lower right — Below the waist, on your opponent's right side. Legs, hip, and flank.

Lower left — Below the waist, on your opponent's left side.

Fiore explicitly numbers these in the Getty manuscript. The master figure in several plays calls out which opening is being taken or covered. This is not an abstraction imposed on the system — it is the system's own vocabulary.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
A guard covers certain openings and leaves others accessible.

Posta di Donna Destra is positioned to threaten the upper openings with a diagonal cut. It naturally covers the upper right while the cut travels. What it leaves open depends on how the opponent responds.

Tutta Porta di Ferro covers the lower openings strongly. The low blade position makes it difficult to target the legs and lower body effectively. But the high openings are more exposed than from an elevated guard.

Understanding this lets you read a guard as information rather than just a posture. If your opponent is in a low guard, the lower openings are covered and the upper ones are available. Your technique choice follows from that read.

docs.title: Openings Are Not Static
docs.text:
An opening that exists at the start of an exchange may close as the technique develops, and a new one may appear.

When the opponent raises their guard to cover the upper right, the lower openings open. When they extend for a thrust, their arm and shoulder are exposed in ways they were not before. When they step offline, the opening that was directly in front of them shifts.

Training to read openings means training to read them as they change, not just at the start of the exchange.

docs.title: Connection to the Fiore Curriculum
docs.text:
In the plays section, the four openings appear constantly without being named explicitly.

The Exchange of Thrusts takes the upper opening opposite to the incoming thrust. The Peasant's Blow exploits the opening created when the opponent's strike carries past center. The pommel strike in stretto targets the face — the upper opening — before the blade wraps to close behind the neck.

Reading those plays through the lens of the four openings makes the logic visible. The plays are not a collection of tricks. They are a set of answers to specific opening-and-cover problems.

docs.title: In Practice
docs.text:
When reviewing a sparring exchange, use the openings as a diagnostic:

  • What opening did the attack target?
  • Was that opening actually available, or did the attacker misread the guard?
  • What opened up as a result of the attack?
  • Did the defender's response close the targeted opening, or did it close a different one while leaving the first exposed?

Most fencing errors reduce to one of two problems: attacking a closed opening, or failing to close the opening being attacked. The four openings make those errors visible and nameable.

docs.title: Fiore for HEMA Fencers
docs.text:
← Back to Materials

Getty MS Ludwig XV 13, folio 22r, c. 1409, "Six masters we are, and we dispute one to another; each does something that the others do not." J. Paul Getty Museum (Open Content)

Author: Chris Mineweaser

An interpretation of the longsword system of Fiore dei Liberi for modern HEMA fencing practice. Drawn from the Getty Manuscript (c. 1409), this material covers guards, the seven blows, and tactical concepts, with commentary focused on competitive and practical application rather than strict historical reconstruction.

docs.title: What's Inside
docs.text:
History — Background on Fiore dei Liberi and the structure of his system, including the Segno and the four virtues.

Glossary — Italian terminology used throughout the curriculum, with definitions and context.

Guards — All twelve guards of the longsword system, with mechanical description, bilateral variations, tactical purpose, and counters.

Strikes — The seven blows of the sword: the two fendente, two mezzani, two sottani, and the thrust.

Plays — Fiore's combat plays (zoghi) organized by game: Gioco Largo (wide play, measure, geometry) and Gioco Stretto (narrow play, joint locks, disarms, throws). Includes the Exchange of Thrusts, Breaking the Thrust, the Peasant's Blow, the three joint locks, disarms, and takedowns, with modern competitive application throughout.

docs.title: Key Principles of Fiore's Art
docs.text: Principle Meaning Guards are not static positions. A guard is a tactical idea, not a pose. Every guard generates actions. Guards naturally produce certain attacks and defenses. Defense creates offense. Covers often become attacks. Threat controls behavior. The opponent must react to credible threats. Measure governs fencing. Distance determines what actions are possible. Structure enables power. Strong alignment creates effective actions. The shortest path arrives first. Efficient actions often defeat larger motions. Control the centerline. Whoever owns the line often owns the exchange. Strike and recover. Every attack should return safely under cover. Force reactions. Make the opponent solve problems rather than executing their own plan.

docs.title: Bicorno
docs.text:
Getty MS Ludwig XV 13, c. 1409 - J. Paul Getty Museum (Open Content)

Flos Duellatorum (Pisani-Dossi MS), c. 1409 - Novati facsimile edition, 1902

The Two-Horned Guard

Classification: Instabile — Mutable Guard

Bicorno is unlike any other guard in Fiore's longsword system. Where every other guard uses a conventional placement of both hands on the handle, Bicorno introduces a reversed grip: both hands remain on the handle, but the front hand grips near the pommel with the palm rotated outward, while both elbows press inward toward each other. The sword is raised overhead, the elbows form the guard's characteristic horned silhouette, and the guard presents two simultaneous threats: the point projecting forward from above, and the pommel ready at close quarters.

For the modern fencer, Bicorno teaches a principle that no other guard addresses so directly: the sword in close combat is not only a blade: it is a lever, a hook, a bludgeon, and a handle. When measure collapses and the blade is too long to swing freely, Bicorno transforms the weapon from a distance tool into a close-quarters control instrument.

Fiore calls it "perfect defense." That precision of language is worth noting. Not strong, not stable, but perfect.

docs.title: Getty Manuscript Text
docs.text: "Bicorno son chiamado, per nome si perfecta, che cum doe punte e cum doe tagli, facio mia defesa perfecta. E posso ferir e defender cum li agudissimi mei corni. E per tal cason io son contra le altre prese ad ogne homo adorni."

docs.title: Translation
docs.text:
"I am called Two-Horned, by name so perfect, for with two points and two edges I make my defense perfect. And I can strike and defend with my very sharp horns. And for this reason I am against the other grips, adorned for every man."

Fiore's description has a different quality from the other guards. He does not describe physical positioning or tactical movement — he declares perfection. Two points and two edges. Perfect defense. Against the other grips.

The language of "horns" is deliberate and precise. The guard does not simply have one threatening point; it has two. And both horns are sharp.

docs.title: The Meaning of the Name
docs.text:
Bicorno means Two-Horned, from the Italian bi (two) and corno (horn).

The name refers to the dual-threat structure of the guard. The first horn is the sword's point, projecting forward from the overhead position. The second horn is the pommel, positioned toward the opponent by the reversed front hand and ready to strike or hook at close distance.

The shape of the guard itself expresses the name: the elbows pressed inward and raised overhead form the arcing silhouette of two horns rising from the body's center. The image is immediate and physical. This is a guard that looks like what it is.

docs.title: The Reversed Grip
docs.text:
Bicorno introduces a grip configuration found nowhere else in Fiore's longsword system.

In the standard grip, both hands hold the handle with the palms facing the same direction. In Bicorno, the front hand is reversed: it grips near the pommel with the palm rotated outward and the elbow pressed inward toward the body's centerline.

This reversed configuration accomplishes several things.

It creates the horned silhouette. The elbows, pressed inward and raised overhead, form the characteristic shape that gives the guard its name. The inward pressure of both elbows produces a strong triangular structure that resists displacement from multiple directions.

It positions the pommel as an active weapon. With the front hand reversed near the pommel, that end of the sword is oriented toward the opponent and can be struck forward or used to hook at close distance.

It locks the overhead structure. The combination of elbows inward and reversed front hand creates a position that is genuinely difficult to press through or displace. This is the "perfect defense" Fiore describes — not because the guard covers every line passively, but because its structure resists interference.

It enables immediate transitions. As an Instabile guard, Bicorno is not meant to be held statically. The reversed grip can release quickly into descending cuts or thrusts as the tactical moment demands.

docs.title: Body Position
docs.text:
The stance is forward-weighted, reflecting the guard's commitment to close-quarters engagement.

The body leans slightly into the overhead structure. Where Posta Corona and Posta Frontale hold the elevated position from a neutral stance, Bicorno's forward weight reflects its role as an entering guard — it is built for the moment when distance has already collapsed and the fencer is in close contact.

docs.title: Hand and Sword Position
docs.text:
Both hands grip the handle: the rear hand in a standard grip, the front hand reversed near the pommel with the palm facing outward.

The sword is raised overhead with both elbows pressed inward toward the centerline. The point projects forward and slightly downward from the overhead position, threatening at roughly the opponent's face height from above.

The elbows form the "horns" of the guard. Their inward pressure creates a compact structural triangle — body, rear arm, front arm — that is very difficult to displace.

The pommel, held by the reversed front hand, remains available for striking or hooking actions at close distance.

docs.title: Tactical Function
docs.text:
Bicorno's tactical identity is built around one specific context: gioco stretto, the narrow play at close distance where the opponent's blade is no longer free to swing.

When two fencers have closed to bind distance and the full cutting arcs of the longsword are no longer available, most guards become difficult to deploy. Bicorno is designed for exactly this range.

Perfect defense through dual threats: With the point projecting from above and the pommel available at close range, the guard covers two lines simultaneously. An opponent trying to find an opening must account for both threats, and the angle of the overhead position makes many attacking lines converge directly on one or the other.

Precision thrusting from above: The overhead position angles the point downward at an unusual approach, arriving from above rather than straight ahead, which makes it difficult to read and hard to parry with a standard guard response. Against armored opponents, this downward angle was critical for finding gaps in the armor around the neck and shoulders.

Against grips and grappling: Fiore says the guard is "against the other grips." When an opponent attempts to grab or control the sword or the fencer's body, the pommel provides immediate counter-options, and the locked structure of the reversed grip makes the fencer's weapon more secure against attempts to disarm.

docs.title: The Two Horns
docs.text:
Understanding both horns of Bicorno as active and simultaneous threats is essential to understanding the guard correctly.

The blade point: Projecting forward from the overhead position, the point threatens the opponent's face, throat, and upper chest. A thrust from this position arrives at an unusual downward angle, which makes it difficult to read and hard to parry with a standard guard response.

The pommel: The reversed front hand near the pommel positions that end of the sword toward the opponent for hooking, striking, or controlling actions. In close quarters where the blade cannot swing freely, the pommel can impact with significant force.

An opponent who tries to close against Bicorno discovers that both ends of the weapon are dangerous. There is no safe angle of approach.

docs.title: Modern Application
docs.text:
In modern fencing, Bicorno is most relevant in two contexts.

As a close-quarters guard: When sparring has closed to bind distance and neither fencer can freely swing, Bicorno provides a structured response rather than scrambling. The locked overhead structure allows the fencer to maintain control, continue threatening with both the point from above and the pommel at close range, and seek the decisive thrust that ends the exchange.

As a pedagogical tool: The reversed grip teaches students that the sword can be held in configurations beyond the standard grip, and that both ends of the weapon can be threatening simultaneously. Practicing Bicorno develops a more complete understanding of the weapon's versatility at close distance.

docs.title: Connection to the Four Virtues
docs.text:
Bicorno most strongly expresses the Elephant.

The reversed grip structure creates a guard that is genuinely very difficult to displace or overwhelm. The inward elbows, the overhead position, and the locked geometry all contribute to a structural solidity that can receive pressure from multiple directions without collapsing. Like the elephant with its castle, Bicorno holds its ground through structural superiority rather than agility.

The Tiger governs the speed of the close-quarters thrust. At narrow play distance, the thrust from Bicorno must arrive before the opponent can reset to a more advantageous position.

The Lynx reads which horn is appropriate — the point thrust or the pommel strike — based on what the opponent is doing and where the opening exists.

The Lion commits to the close-quarters range that Bicorno requires. Maintaining the guard at narrow play distance, with the opponent immediately present, demands courage and willingness to engage at the most demanding measure.

docs.title: Defeating the Guard
docs.text:
Bicorno is most vulnerable before close distance is established.

At standard measure, where full cutting arcs are available, the overhead grip sacrifices the flexibility that a standard grip provides. An opponent who can maintain or create distance prevents the guard from reaching its optimal range.

The guard is also less effective against opponents with superior physical strength who can use grappling or wrestling actions to overwhelm the overhead structure before the point or pommel can be brought to bear.

Finally, the overhead structure leaves the lower body and legs more exposed than most guards. An opponent who directs attacks low, while staying outside the immediate threat of the two horns, can find lines that Bicorno does not naturally cover.

docs.title: What This Guard Is Not For
docs.text:
Bicorno is not a guard for middle or long measure. Its design is explicitly for close quarters — the narrow play where other guards have lost their primary function.

It is also not a guard for wide cutting actions. The reversed grip and locked elbow structure limit the available cutting arcs. The guard favors controlled thrusting and close-quarters control over large rotational cuts.

Finally, the guard requires familiarity with the reversed grip before it can be used confidently. Students should practice the grip structure and locked elbow position in isolation before attempting to apply it in dynamic exchanges.

docs.title: Drill 1 — Establishing the Reversed Grip
docs.text:
Begin by establishing the reversed grip: rear hand on the handle with a standard grip, front hand reversed near the pommel with the palm facing outward.

Raise the sword overhead, pressing both elbows inward toward the centerline. The point projects forward from the overhead position.

A partner applies gentle downward pressure on the blade from above. Maintain the structure without the elbows collapsing outward or the reversed front hand losing its connection.

Hold for ten seconds, then rest. Repeat five times. Focus on the structural triangle formed by the elbows and sword, and the feeling of the locked overhead position.

docs.title: Drill 2 — The Overhead Thrust
docs.text:
From Bicorno, step forward and deliver a controlled thrust downward and forward from the overhead position.

The point drops toward a specific target — a partner holds their fist at face height as a reference point.

The overhead starting position gives the thrust an unusual downward angle. Repeat ten times, adjusting the target height: face, throat, chest.

The goal is to experience the distinctive angle of the overhead thrust and the way the locked structure guides the point forward.

docs.title: Drill 3 — Defending the Overhead
docs.text:
One fencer assumes Bicorno. The partner delivers a slow descending Fendente toward the Bicorno fencer's head.

The Bicorno fencer uses the overhead structure — the elevated blade and locked elbows — to receive the descending strike, allowing it to contact the blade and be deflected.

After the deflection, the Bicorno fencer immediately delivers a controlled thrust downward from the overhead position.

Repeat ten times, then switch roles. The lesson: the overhead structure in Bicorno is not passive — it catches the incoming strike and immediately creates the opportunity for a counter-thrust.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
The most common mistake is allowing the elbows to collapse outward rather than pressing them inward. The elbows-in position is what creates the structural lock and the characteristic "horned" shape. Elbows pointing outward convert Bicorno into an unstable overhead position with no structural advantage.

Another frequent error is reversing the wrong hand or gripping too loosely with the front hand. The front hand should be near the pommel, fully reversed with the palm facing outward, and with enough grip to maintain control under pressure.

Some students also treat Bicorno as a purely defensive guard and fail to use both horns actively. The point must remain threatening forward, and the pommel must remain available as a striking or hooking option. Focusing only on one horn misses the guard's defining characteristic.

Finally, hunched or collapsed shoulders reduce the structural integrity of the overhead position. The shoulders should be relaxed and the structure open — tension in the upper body creates rigidity that slows response.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text:
Bicorno is the guard of the narrow space.

It carries two threats simultaneously — the point projecting from above, the pommel ready below — and holds them both through the locked structure of the reversed grip. Against attempts to control, grab, or overwhelm at close distance, it presents a problem with two sharp answers.

Perfect defense does not mean perfect coverage of every line. It means that wherever the opponent reaches, one horn is already there.

docs.title: Coda Longa
docs.text:
Flos Duellatorum (Pisani-Dossi MS), c. 1409 - Novati facsimile edition, 1902

The Long Tail

Classification: Stabile — Stable Guard

Coda Longa is one of the most deceptive guards in the Getty manuscript of Fiore dei Liberi. The sword trails behind the body like the tail of an animal, appearing withdrawn, passive, or even vulnerable.

But this appearance is intentional.

Fiore describes Coda Longa as a false guard, a position designed to mislead the opponent about your true intentions. Although the posture appears defensive, the guard is built for sudden offense, explosive upward strikes, and aggressive entry.

For the modern fencer, Coda Longa teaches an essential tactical principle:

What appears weak may actually be preparing to attack.

This guard is not about waiting safely behind structure. It is about baiting the opponent into overcommitting before launching forward with explosive motion.

This chapter treats Coda Longa Destra and Coda Longa Sinestra together, since both versions express the same tactical idea through mirrored body mechanics.

docs.title: Getty Manuscript Text
docs.text: "Questa si e posta di coda longa ch'e destesa in terra di dredo, ella po metter punta, e denançi po covrir e ferire. E se ello passa inançi e tra del fendente, in Lo zogo stretto entra senza fallimento, che tal guardia e bona per aspettare, che de quella in altre tosto po intrare."

docs.title: Translation
docs.text:
“This is the guard of the Long Tail which is extended to the ground behind [you]. It can make a thrust, and to the front it can cover and strike. And if [the fencer] passes forward and enters with a Fendente, [he] enters into the narrow play without fail. For such a guard is good for waiting, and from it [the fencer] can quickly enter into other [guards].”

This description immediately establishes the identity of the guard.

Coda Longa is deceptive by nature. Its purpose is not simply to defend, but to disguise offensive intent behind an apparently withdrawn posture.

docs.title: The Meaning of the Name
docs.text:
The name “Coda Longa,” or “Long Tail,” refers to the sword trailing behind the body like the tail of an animal.

The blade extends rearward while the body appears withdrawn and defensive.

This creates two important effects:

  • the opponent often underestimates the immediate threat
  • the sword gains a long path for acceleration into rising attacks

Like many of Fiore’s guards, the name describes both the shape of the position and the tactical behavior associated with it.

docs.title: Right and Left Variations
docs.text:
Like many guards in Fiore’s system, Coda Longa exists on both the right and left sides of the body.

Although the tactical purpose remains the same, the angle of acceleration and entry changes significantly between the two versions.

docs.title: Coda Longa Destra
docs.text:
In the right-side variation, the sword trails behind the right side of the body while the right foot remains forward.

The guard naturally launches:

  • sottano destra
  • rising thrusts from the right side
  • explosive diagonal entries

This is the version most commonly associated with the classic Getty image of Coda Longa.

docs.title: Coda Longa Sinestra
docs.text:
In the left-side variation, the sword trails behind the left side of the body, with the left foot typically forward and the body withdrawn to the right.

This version naturally generates:

  • Sottano Sinestra, the rising cut from the left
  • Rising thrusts from the left line
  • Explosive diagonal entry from the left side

The angle of acceleration and the line of attack feel noticeably different from the Destra version. The rising cut from the left approaches the opponent’s right side, a line they may be less practiced at defending, and the entry angle shifts enough to require genuine bilateral training rather than simple mirroring.

The false guard concept applies equally on both sides. An opponent who has learned to read Coda Longa Destra as a bait may not immediately recognize the same trap presented from the left. Training Sinestra with equal intention gives the guard a second deceptive dimension.

Training both sides reinforces Fiore’s bilateral approach and prevents the guard from becoming predictable or one-sided.

docs.title: Physical Structure
docs.text: Coda Longa is held low and withdrawn with the sword positioned behind the body.

docs.title: Body Position
docs.text:
The stance is typically rear-weighted with the lead foot forward and the body slightly withdrawn.

The posture should feel balanced and mobile rather than collapsed or retreating.

Although the guard appears defensive, the body remains prepared to drive explosively forward.

The guard should feel loaded.

docs.title: Hand and Sword Position
docs.text:
The hands are held low near or behind the hip while the sword trails behind the body.

The blade should feel chambered rather than abandoned.

This is important.

The guard is not passive. The sword is deeply loaded behind the body in preparation for rapid upward or forward motion.

The posture should resemble a compressed spring.

docs.title: Upper Hand vs Lower Hand Hip Connection
docs.text:
One of the most important structural details in Coda Longa is which hand remains closest to the hip during the chamber.

Small changes here significantly affect:

  • distance
  • leverage
  • acceleration
  • structure

docs.title: Lower Hand Connected to the Hip
docs.text:
When the lower hand remains structurally connected to the hip, the sword becomes strongly integrated into the body.

This creates:

  • stronger mechanical structure
  • greater rotational power
  • more explosive acceleration
  • better whole-body connection

The strike tends to feel:

  • compact
  • heavy
  • powerful

This version often sacrifices a small amount of reach in exchange for stronger body-driven mechanics.

For many fencers, this creates the clearest “loaded spring” feeling.

docs.title: Upper Hand Connected to the Hip
docs.text:
When the upper hand remains closer to the hip while the lower hand extends farther away, the sword gains additional reach and extension.

This creates:

  • longer striking distance
  • wider acceleration arc
  • increased reach during the release

However, the structure often feels:

  • less compact
  • slightly less mechanically powerful
  • more dependent on timing and distance

This variation can produce very fast and difficult-to-read rising attacks, but may feel less structurally stable under pressure.

docs.title: Tactical Implications
docs.text:
The difference between these structures reflects an important principle in Fiore’s system:

Greater extension often increases reach and unpredictability, while tighter body connection increases structure and power.

Neither version is universally correct.

Different fencers may emphasize:

  • explosive power
  • deceptive reach
  • structural stability
  • acceleration

depending on:

  • body type
  • tactical preference
  • weapon length
  • fencing context

The important thing is that the guard remains mechanically connected and prepared to explode forward.

docs.title: Tactical Function
docs.text:
Coda Longa is a guard of deception, invitation, and explosive offense.

The position encourages the opponent to believe:

  • you are retreating
  • you are vulnerable
  • you are defending passively
  • you have surrendered initiative

In reality:

  • the sword is deeply chambered
  • the body is prepared to advance
  • the guard is built to launch explosive attacks

This is not a waiting guard.

It is a trap.

docs.title: The False Guard Concept
docs.text:
Coda Longa is one of the clearest expressions of deception in Fiore’s system.

A false guard deliberately communicates misleading information to the opponent.

The guard appears weak while secretly preparing strength.

This is an important tactical lesson for modern fencing.

Opponents frequently react not only to actual threats, but also to perceived intent. Coda Longa manipulates those expectations by disguising offense as defense.

The guard invites the opponent to attack first, then punishes their commitment with sudden acceleration.

docs.title: The Natural Strike — Sottano
docs.text:
Because the sword begins low and withdrawn, the most natural action from Coda Longa is a rising cut, or sottano.

From the rearward chamber, the blade whips upward from below in a long accelerating arc.

This rising action is difficult to read because much of the blade’s motion begins behind the body and outside the opponent’s immediate line of sight.

The strike emerges suddenly and violently from below.

docs.title: The Rising Sottano into High Guards
docs.text:
One of the defining actions of Coda Longa is the explosive transition from low withdrawal into high offensive positions.

As the sword launches upward in a rising sottano, the blade naturally finishes in high guards such as:

  • Posta di Fenestra
  • Posta Frontale

This creates a powerful tactical transition:

  • withdrawn deception
  • explosive rising attack
  • immediate high-line pressure

The motion should feel continuous and accelerating rather than segmented into separate actions.

docs.title: Drawing the Attack
docs.text:
One of the primary tactical purposes of Coda Longa is to provoke commitment from the opponent.

Because the guard appears defensive or withdrawn, opponents are often tempted to:

  • rush forward
  • overextend
  • attempt to seize initiative
  • attack aggressively into perceived weakness

Coda Longa exploits this reaction.

As the opponent commits, the guard launches upward into the opening created by their forward motion.

This is why Fiore describes the guard as both deceptive and aggressive simultaneously.

docs.title: Modern Application
docs.text:
In modern fencing, Coda Longa is often underestimated because it appears unconventional and vulnerable.

In practice, however, it can be extremely effective for:

  • baiting aggressive opponents
  • disguising upward attacks
  • creating explosive acceleration
  • forcing reactions through deceptive posture

The guard is especially useful against opponents who:

  • overcommit aggressively
  • chase perceived openings
  • attack recklessly into withdrawn positions

For modern practitioners, the greatest value of Coda Longa lies in understanding how posture and intention influence an opponent’s decisions.

docs.title: Connection to the Four Virtues
docs.text:
Coda Longa strongly expresses the Lynx.

The guard depends on observation, timing, and deception. The fencer studies the opponent and invites a reaction before striking.

The Tiger appears in the explosive acceleration of the release. The Elephant appears in the stable rear-weighted structure. The Lion appears in the courage required to stand in an apparently vulnerable position.

docs.title: Defeating the Guard
docs.text:
Coda Longa is strongest when opponents attack impulsively into its invitation.

The guard becomes less effective when the opponent:

  • refuses to overcommit
  • controls distance carefully
  • pressures safely from above
  • forces the guard to act first

Because the sword begins deeply chambered behind the body, interrupting the rising action early can limit the guard’s effectiveness.

Maintaining measured pressure while denying explosive entry is often more effective than rushing aggressively into the trap.

docs.title: What This Guard Is Not For
docs.text:
Coda Longa is not designed for passive retreating.

Although the posture appears defensive, the guard functions through aggressive offensive intent.

It is also not intended for static point control or prolonged centerline dominance. The sword begins withdrawn rather than extended.

Finally, the guard should not become rigid or visibly tense. Its effectiveness depends heavily on relaxed explosiveness and deceptive presentation.

docs.title: Training the Guard
docs.text: The following drills develop the deceptive posture, explosive release, and tactical timing of Coda Longa.

docs.title: Drill 1 — Establishing the False Guard
docs.text:
Begin in Coda Longa with the body withdrawn and the sword trailing behind the hip.

Maintain relaxed structure while preserving readiness to move explosively forward.

The posture should appear passive while still feeling balanced and prepared.

Have a partner observe the posture and describe what intentions it appears to communicate.

docs.title: Drill 2 — Explosive Sottano
docs.text:
From Coda Longa, step forward explosively and launch a rising sottano from below.

Allow the blade to travel naturally upward into Posta di Fenestra or Posta Frontale.

Focus on:

  • acceleration
  • relaxed release
  • whole-body motion
  • continuous upward flow

Practice from both Destra and Sinestra.

docs.title: Drill 3 — Baiting the Attack
docs.text:
Begin with one fencer in Coda Longa and the other in an aggressive guard such as Donna or Fenestra.

The fencer in Coda Longa presents apparent vulnerability while maintaining readiness to launch forward.

As the opponent commits to attack, explode upward with the rising sottano.

The goal is to intercept the opponent during their forward commitment.

docs.title: Drill 4 — Continuous Offensive Flow
docs.text:
Launch a rising sottano from Coda Longa into a high guard.

From the high position, immediately continue into a descending fendente before recovering.

This drill develops the flowing offensive transitions naturally generated by the guard.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
A common mistake is becoming genuinely defensive in the guard. Coda Longa only functions correctly when the offensive intention remains active.

Another issue is visibly tensing before the strike. Telegraphing the attack reduces the effectiveness of the deception.

Some students also fail to withdraw the body sufficiently. The deeper the chamber and withdrawal, the greater the explosive potential of the release.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text:
Coda Longa is a guard of hidden offense.

It appears defensive while secretly preparing explosive attack.

Like the release of a compressed spring, the sword launches upward from behind the body with sudden acceleration, punishing opponents who mistake apparent weakness for true vulnerability.

docs.title: Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana
docs.text:
Getty MS Ludwig XV 13, c. 1409 - J. Paul Getty Museum (Open Content)

Flos Duellatorum (Pisani-Dossi MS), c. 1409 - Novati facsimile edition, 1902

a24eaade0ab41ac3fd6b7ace50609487060eef34 The Middle Boar's Tusk ======= The Middle Boar's Tooth Stashed changes

Classification: Stabile — Stable Guard

Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana is the centered expression of the boar's tooth guard family. Where Dente di Zenghiaro Destra and Sinestra angle the sword to one side or the other, Mezzana holds the point on the body's centerline at mid-body height, facing directly forward. The guard occupies the middle position between its two siblings — the tooth that splits the center.

For the modern fencer, Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana teaches a principle of defensive patience: the guard that faces directly forward, threatening nothing and yielding nothing, can receive any attack on either line and answer it with the same rising defense. It is not a guard of deception or declaration. It is a guard of stillness that contains violence.

Fiore treats the three Dente di Zenghiaro positions as expressions of the same fundamental idea. The Mezzana is the middle of the three, and what either outer tooth can do, the middle tooth can also do.

docs.title: Translation
docs.text:
"This is Posta Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana, because there are two teeth in the whole boar, and the middle tooth shares the nature of both. What one tooth can do, the other tooth can do, and what the other tooth can do, the middle tooth can do also."

Fiore's description emphasizes continuity and shared capability across the three positions. The Mezzana is not lesser than the outer positions — it contains both of them. A fencer who understands this guard understands the entire boar's tooth family.

The verse also carries a tactical implication: the Mezzana can answer from either line. Attacks to the right fall to the response of the Destra; attacks to the left fall to the response of the Sinestra. From the center, the fencer does not commit to one side before the attack arrives.

docs.title: The Meaning of the Name
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro means Boar's Tooth, and Mezzana means Middle or Median.

The full name describes the position precisely: the middle tooth of the boar, centered between the two outer teeth. Where Destra places the sword on the right hip and Sinestra places it on the left, Mezzana holds it along the centerline of the body.

The boar's tooth image is about the way the sword rises. A boar does not swing its tusks in horizontal arcs — it drives them upward from below in a sudden, committed thrust. The tooth rises from low to high with the full weight of the animal behind it. The Mezzana expresses this in its most direct form: a rising false-edge cut straight up the center.

docs.title: Relationship to Dente di Zenghiaro Destra and Sinestra
docs.text:
The three Dente di Zenghiaro guards are a family, not three separate guards.

Destra and Sinestra angle the sword to one side, creating a chamber for the rising sottano from that side. Mezzana holds the center — the point forward, the sword integrated into the body's midline, neither angled left nor right.

In practical use, a fencer moving between guards will often pass through Mezzana as they cross from one side to the other. It is also the natural arrival point for attacks that come directly down the center, where neither the Destra nor Sinestra response is better positioned.

The shared capability Fiore describes means that the Mezzana is not a compromise — it is a complete position that happens to sit at the center.

docs.title: Body Position
docs.text:
The stance is rear-weighted, with the weight settled back and the lead foot forward.

The posture remains upright rather than crouched. The weight settling rearward allows the body to drive forward explosively when the rising sottano is launched, converting the stored energy of the rear-weighted stance into forward acceleration.

The guard should feel grounded and calm. There is no angling of the body to either side. The fencer faces the opponent directly with the centerline aligned.

docs.title: Hand and Sword Position
docs.text:
The hands are held at approximately hip to low-waist height, close to the body.

The sword aligns along the body's centerline with the point projecting forward at mid-body level. Neither the blade nor the grip angles toward either side — this is the distinguishing feature of Mezzana against the outer variants.

The elbows remain connected to the sides of the body, keeping the weapon structurally integrated rather than extended forward like Posta Longa. The point projects forward with enough presence to threaten, but the guard's strength comes from the structural connection of the weapon to the body, not from extension.

The false edge is oriented to face upward, positioning it for the diagonal rising cut that defines the guard's primary attack.

docs.title: Tactical Function
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana operates through defensive patience and explosive rising response.

Holding the center: The forward-facing centerline position gives the guard no obvious left or right commitment. An opponent trying to read the guard finds no angle to exploit. The fencer can answer equally well from either side, and the guard gives away nothing about which response will come.

The rising false-edge sottano: The guard's primary attack is a diagonal rising cut with the false edge — a sottano that travels upward from the low center position toward the opponent's hands, arms, or face. The false edge orientation means the cut rises from below with the back edge leading, creating an unusual angle that many defensive covers are not positioned to handle cleanly.

Defensive interception: Because the point sits on the centerline at mid-body height, it naturally threatens any incoming attack that travels down the center. An opponent charging forward with a direct thrust or descending fendente drives into the guard's point rather than finding an opening beside it. The guard does not need to move to create this threat — it is already in the line.

docs.title: The False Edge Rising Cut
docs.text:
The defining attack of Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana is the sottano with the false (back) edge.

From the low centered position, the false edge faces upward. When the fencer launches the rising cut, the blade travels diagonally upward with the back edge leading, arriving at the opponent's hands or arms from below and inside their guard's coverage.

This approach is tactically significant for two reasons.

First, most defensive covers are oriented to receive the true edge. A rising true-edge sottano from below has a predictable line that experienced fencers defend against habitually. The false-edge variant arrives from a slightly different angle and with a different blade orientation, requiring a distinct defensive response that many fencers have not drilled as thoroughly.

Second, the false edge cut from the centered position travels up the middle — not from either side, but straight through the center. Against an opponent whose guard protects either side without covering the direct middle line, the Mezzana's central path finds the gap between those protections.

docs.title: Modern Application
docs.text:
In modern fencing, Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana is the most statically threatening of the boar's tooth positions.

A fencer who holds the Mezzana against an aggressive opponent presents no commitment to either side, no false opening to exploit, and a direct forward threat that must be respected. Opponents who advance carelessly into the forward point without addressing it find the exchange happens on the Mezzana fencer's terms.

The guard is also useful as a resting position in the middle of a flow. After a descending fendente that clears one side, the fencer can settle momentarily into Mezzana before the next commitment, taking stock of the exchange without leaving an obvious opening.

For students building guard fluency, the Mezzana provides a useful reference point: it is the neutral version of the boar's tooth family, and the transitions from Mezzana to Destra and Sinestra teach the fundamental mechanics of chambering and releasing the rising cut from both sides.

docs.title: Connection to the Four Virtues
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana expresses the Elephant most fully.

The centered, rear-weighted stance with the point forward creates structural stability that is difficult to displace. The guard does not reach toward the opponent; it holds the line and waits. This quality of settled, patient solidity is the Elephant's fortitude expressed as posture.

The Tiger governs the explosive release of the rising sottano. The rear-weighted stance stores energy; the Tiger converts it into sudden forward acceleration when the moment arrives.

The Lynx reads the opponent's line and decides whether the response is the Mezzana's own central rising cut or a shift into the angled response of Destra or Sinestra. The decision must be made as the attack develops, not after it has arrived.

The Lion holds the exposed centerline position. Facing the opponent directly without angling away requires the courage to present oneself honestly to incoming attacks.

docs.title: Defeating the Guard
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana is most vulnerable to attacks that change angle before arriving.

Because the guard holds the center and the false-edge sottano rises through the middle, a committed attack that begins centrally but angles to one side before contact can find the space beside the point rather than running into it.

Indirect approaches — feints that draw the guard's rising response, then redirect into the opening created — also challenge the Mezzana. If the fencer releases the rising cut too early against a feint, the guard is momentarily depleted and the opponent can exploit the recovery.

At very close measure, the low and compact position leaves the head and upper body without the overhead coverage that guards like Bicorno or Posta Frontale provide. An opponent who collapses distance suddenly and drives forward high can find the guard unprepared for the transition to narrow play.

docs.title: What This Guard Is Not For
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana is not a guard for controlling long measure. Its point threatens the mid-body line, not the extended forward space that Posta Longa commands. An opponent at long measure who advances carefully will not be threatened by the guard's forward point until they have already closed to mid range.

It is also not a guard for deception. Unlike Coda Longa, which hides its sword behind the body to disguise intent, Mezzana presents the sword forward and honestly. Its tactical advantage comes from the false-edge cut's unusual angle, not from deceiving the opponent about the position of the blade.

Finally, the guard should not become passive waiting. The settled quality of Mezzana is a readiness to respond, not an absence of threat. The point must remain present and credible, and the fencer must remain genuinely prepared to launch the rising cut rather than simply standing in the position.

docs.title: Drill 1 — Establishing the Mezzana Center
docs.text:
Begin in Dente di Zenghiaro Destra. Transition smoothly to Mezzana by bringing the sword from the right hip to the body's centerline, the point continuing to project forward at mid-body height.

Hold the Mezzana position for fifteen seconds while a partner slowly circles at mid range.

The partner attempts to read which side the rising cut will come from. The fencer does not telegraph any commitment. The point should feel genuinely neutral — capable of rising from either side equally.

Repeat from the Sinestra side, then practice transitioning Destra → Mezzana → Sinestra as a single fluid flow.

docs.title: Drill 2 — The Central Rising Sottano
docs.text:
From Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana, launch a false-edge rising sottano directly up the center.

The false edge leads. The blade travels diagonally upward from the low center, the back edge arriving first toward the partner's extended hand or forearm.

Focus on the path of the blade: it should rise through the centerline, not curve to either side. Repeat ten times.

With a partner: the partner extends a hand at approximately waist height as a target. The Mezzana fencer rises the sottano so the false edge arrives at the underside of the partner's hand. Neither fencer should contact with force — the drill establishes the correct line and angle of the rising cut.

docs.title: Drill 3 — Defensive Interception
docs.text:
One fencer holds Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana. The partner delivers a slow, direct descending fendente aimed at the Mezzana fencer's head.

The Mezzana fencer does not move — the forward point simply receives the incoming blade as the descending cut drives into the guard's structure.

After the contact, the Mezzana fencer steps forward and delivers the rising sottano as a counter.

The lesson: the guard's centered forward point intercepts direct attacks without active deflection. The fencer does not need to block the incoming strike — the position has already occupied the line.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
The most common mistake is allowing the sword to drift off the centerline, angling to one side. Mezzana is defined by its centered position. Any drift toward Destra or Sinestra changes the tactical character of the guard and loses the central threat.

Another error is pointing the false edge incorrectly. The back edge must face upward to allow the rising false-edge cut. Rotating the sword so the true edge faces upward converts the guard into a different position and removes the distinctive false-edge angle of the Mezzana's primary attack.

Some students also allow the stance to become front-weighted, removing the explosive launching potential of the rear-weighted chamber. The guard must feel loaded — the weight back, ready to drive forward — not settled passively on the front foot.

Finally, holding the guard with a rigid, extended point that does not threaten credibly undermines its defensive value. The point must sit forward with genuine presence, close enough to body structure to be supported, far enough forward to require the opponent's attention.

docs.title: Key Idea
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro Mezzana is the guard of the patient center.

It holds the middle, commits to neither side, and carries the same rising tooth that its siblings carry — available from any angle, threatening from no particular direction until the moment it is no longer patient.

What one tooth can do, the middle tooth can also do. But only the middle tooth can wait without choosing.

docs.title: Dente di Zenghiaro Destra and Sinestra
docs.text:
Getty MS Ludwig XV 13, c. 1409 - J. Paul Getty Museum (Open Content)

Flos Duellatorum (Pisani-Dossi MS), c. 1409 - Novati facsimile edition, 1902

a24eaade0ab41ac3fd6b7ace50609487060eef34 The Wild Boar’s Tusk ======= The Wild Boar’s Tooth Stashed changes

Classification: Stabile — Stable Guard

Dente di Zenghiaro is one of the most aggressive low guards in the Getty manuscript of Fiore dei Liberi. Although held low near the hip, the guard is not passive or withdrawn. Like the strike of a wild boar, it rises suddenly upward before returning downward with force.

For the modern fencer, Dente di Zenghiaro teaches an essential principle of Fiore’s system: a guard may appear defensive while still threatening immediate offense. The sword waits low, hidden beneath the line of engagement, ready to thrust upward into the opponent before descending again into control.

Unlike high guards such as Posta di Donna, which generate descending force from above, Dente di Zenghiaro generates power from below through rising actions, thrusts, and returning cuts.

This chapter treats Dente di Zenghiaro Destra and Sinestra together, since the tactical principles remain the same on both sides of the body.

docs.title: Getty Manuscript Text
docs.text: "Questo si e dente di zenghiaro perche a modo di senghiaro sie feri. E talvolta fa le punte forti de sotto in suso ala faza senza passare del pe. E torna cum la punta per la medesima strada cum rebatter fendente ale braze. Anchora fa la punta alta ala faza cum lo pe denanci passando fora de strada. E torna cum fendenti ala testa e ale braze e torna in sua guardia. E subito tira un'altra punta cum lo pe denanci passando fora de strada. E questa guardia si po fare bona defensa in zogho stretto."

docs.title: Translation
docs.text:
“This is the Boar’s Tooth, because it strikes the way the wild boar strikes. Sometimes it makes powerful thrusts from below up into the face, without stepping forward, and it returns along the same path with a downward strike to the arms. Other times as it thrusts the point of the sword high into the face, it advances the front foot forwards, then returns to its guard with a downward strike to the head or the arms. Then it quickly launches another thrust with another advance of the front foot. And this guard can mount a good defense against the Narrow Game.”

This description gives one of the clearest examples in Fiore’s system of a guard being defined through its tactical behavior rather than simply its shape.

Dente di Zenghiaro is active, probing, and cyclical. It rises upward into attack, returns downward into control, and immediately launches again.

docs.title: The Meaning of the Name
docs.text:
The name “Dente di Zenghiaro,” or “Wild Boar’s Tooth,” describes both the shape and behavior of the guard.

Like the tooth of a boar, the sword rises violently upward from below. Fiore specifically compares the action of the guard to the way a wild boar strikes.

This comparison is important.

The guard does not remain statically threatening with the point elevated toward the opponent. Instead, its threat emerges through sudden upward motion from a low position.

The sword rises into the attack, then returns downward along the same path.

docs.title: Right and Left Variations
docs.text: Like many positions in Fiore’s system, Dente di Zenghiaro can be expressed on both sides of the body.

docs.title: Dente di Zenghiaro Destra
docs.text:
In the right-side variation, the sword is held low near the right hip with the point projecting forward from below.

The rear hand remains structurally connected to the hip while the forward hand guides the blade.

From this position, the sword naturally rises upward into thrusts and sottani from the right side.

docs.title: Dente di Zenghiaro Sinestra
docs.text:
The left-side variation mirrors the same structure on the opposite side of the body.

The sword is held low near the left hip, allowing the blade to rise upward from the left side.

Mechanically, the tactical principles remain identical, though the angle of attack and interception changes.

Training both sides reinforces an important principle in Fiore’s art: guards are part of a connected bilateral system rather than isolated poses.

docs.title: Physical Structure
docs.text: Dente di Zenghiaro is held low and compact with the sword connected closely to the body.

docs.title: Body Position
docs.text:
The stance is grounded and balanced, usually with slight forward pressure through the lead foot.

The body remains upright and stable while the sword waits beneath the primary line of engagement.

Unlike guards such as Fenestra, which project immediate point threat, Dente di Zenghiaro conceals much of its offense below the opponent’s line of sight.

docs.title: Hand and Sword Position
docs.text:
The hands are held low near the hip.

In the Getty manuscript, the point is generally shown projecting forward or slightly downward rather than dramatically elevated upward.

This is important.

The guard is not defined by a permanently raised point, but by its ability to rise suddenly into thrusts and ascending actions.

The sword should feel grounded, compact, and ready to spring upward.

docs.title: Tactical Function
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro is a guard of probing offense, rising attacks, and immediate continuation.

Fiore explicitly describes the guard launching thrusts upward into the opponent’s face before returning downward with cuts to the arms or head.

The guard naturally chains actions together:

  • upward thrust
  • returning downward strike
  • immediate renewed thrust

This creates a cyclical rhythm of attack and recovery.

Because the sword begins low, the upward thrust can emerge unexpectedly beneath the opponent’s line of defense.

Fiore also notes that the guard performs well in the Narrow Game, meaning it remains effective even at closer distances where structure and compact actions become critical.

docs.title: Probing and Pressure
docs.text:
Fiore describes Dente di Zenghiaro as probing against all guards.

This is an important tactical idea.

The guard constantly tests openings through upward thrusts and rising pressure. Rather than waiting passively, it pressures the opponent into reacting.

The low position makes these attacks difficult to read clearly, especially when combined with quick recoveries and repeated thrusts.

This is a stable guard, but not a passive one.

docs.title: Rising Actions and the Sottano
docs.text:
Because Dente di Zenghiaro begins low, it naturally generates rising actions such as sottani.

From Dente di Zenghiaro Destra, the sword rises upward from the right side (Sottano Destra). From Dente di Zenghiaro Sinestra, the sword rises upward from the left (Sottano Sinestra).

These ascending actions are especially effective for:

  • attacking the hands and arms
  • intercepting descending attacks
  • lifting the opponent’s structure
  • entering safely into close distance

This reinforces an important principle in Fiore’s system:

High guards naturally generate descending blows. Low guards naturally generate rising blows.

docs.title: The Rising Thrust into Returning Fendente
docs.text:
One of the defining tactical patterns of Dente di Zenghiaro is the transition from rising thrust into descending strike.

Fiore specifically describes:

  • thrusting upward into the face
  • returning downward along the same path
  • striking the arms or head with a fendente

This creates a continuous offensive cycle.

The sword rises suddenly from below, forcing the opponent to react upward. As their structure lifts, the blade immediately returns downward into the exposed upper openings.

The motion should feel fluid and uninterrupted rather than divided into separate actions.

docs.title: Defense in the Narrow Game
docs.text:
Fiore notes that Dente di Zenghiaro “can mount a good defense against the Narrow Game.”

This reflects the compact structure of the guard.

Because the hands remain close to the body and the actions travel efficiently upward and downward, the guard remains functional even when distance collapses and larger motions become difficult.

The guard’s structure allows it to defend, strike, and recover quickly at close measure.

docs.title: Modern Application
docs.text:
In modern fencing, Dente di Zenghiaro is often misunderstood as either purely defensive or overly static.

The Getty manuscript suggests something much more dynamic.

The guard functions through:

  • upward probing thrusts
  • quick returning cuts
  • repeated offensive pressure
  • compact structure

For modern practitioners, the greatest value of Dente di Zenghiaro lies in its ability to attack from below the opponent’s expectations while maintaining strong structural control.

The mirrored left-side variation is especially important in modern fencing because it prevents the system from becoming one-sided. Both directions must be trained equally.

docs.title: Connection to the Four Virtues
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro strongly expresses the Elephant.

Its grounded structure and compact positioning give the guard stability during repeated actions.

The Tiger appears in the sudden acceleration of the upward thrust. The Lynx appears in the probing nature of the attacks and the reading of openings. The Lion appears in the commitment to entering upward into the opponent’s line.

docs.title: Defeating the Guard
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro is strongest when allowed to attack upward from below the line.

Opponents who keep their hands extended or high often become vulnerable to the rising thrusts and returning cuts of the guard.

To challenge Dente di Zenghiaro effectively, it is often necessary to:

  • force the guard laterally
  • disrupt its rhythm
  • pressure it before the rising action begins
  • control the line from above

Because the guard relies on cyclical motion, interrupting the transition between rising and descending actions can limit its effectiveness.

docs.title: What This Guard Is Not For
docs.text:
Dente di Zenghiaro is not designed for large sweeping motions or wide rotational strikes.

Its strength lies in compact efficiency.

It is also not intended to remain completely static. Although stable in structure, the guard functions through continuous probing pressure.

Finally, the guard should not be exaggerated into an overly elevated “point-up” posture. The Getty manuscript consistently depicts a lower, more grounded position whose threat emerges through motion.

docs.title: Training the Guard
docs.text: The following drills develop the structure, rising actions, and tactical rhythm of Dente di Zenghiaro.

docs.title: Drill 1 — Establishing Structure
docs.text:
Begin in Dente di Zenghiaro with the sword held low near the hip.
